data.name: Juan Musso
data.description: Musso’s career has been marked by progress from a very young age, even before he reached the level of maturity typically associated with goalkeepers. After taking his first steps at a local club in his hometown, San Nicolás, he joined Racing’s youth academy at 18. Two years later he won the league as the third-choice goalkeeper and made his official debut at 21. A season later, Udinese took a gamble on his potential and it paid off: three years later they quintupled their money as he moved to Atalanta for €20m. He is now the backup for Jan Oblak at Atlético Madrid, and increasingly looks a capable replacement: this season, with Oblak sidelined with a muscle strain, Musso became the first goalkeeper this century to keep a clean sheet in each of his first four appearances in La Liga (though he then shipped seven goals in his next three). “He is an important figure within the squad and the dressing room,” Diego Simeone says. “He brings energy wherever it’s needed and contributes much more than just his goalkeeping skills.”

data.name: Marcos Senesi
data.description: Senesi's faith paid off. In 2022 he was approached by Roberto Mancini to join the Italy squad but declined, staying true to his dream of representing Argentina. Now, as a late replacement for the injured Leonardo Balerdi, he has made it to a World Cup with them. The ball-playing left-footed centre-back first moved to Europe with Feyenoord in 2019, then to England with Bournemouth in 2022. After 128 appearances, he joined Tottenham on a free as part of Roberto De Zerbi's rebuild just before this tournament. He's a big fan of tattoos – with one, on his right arm, reading "Only God can judge me”– and his partner is Kelci Rose Bowers, who played for Bournemouth's women's team until her deal expired this summer.

data.name: Nicolás Tagliafico
data.description: He is one of the few remaining players Lionel Scaloni inherited from the 2018 World Cup squad. In time Tagliafico, through consistency and a high level of performance, has become a key figure. Having come through the ranks at Banfield, the left-back is known for his total dedication, commitment, and tireless work rate. On social media, as well as on his own streaming channels, he often shows his family side alongside his partner, the influencer Caro Calvagni, and their two French bulldogs, Galo and Pepu. He also reveals a more intellectual side, showing interest in a wide range of topics beyond football: his favourite hobby is drawing, of which he says: “Concentrating on just one thing helps to clear the mind.” After successful spells at Independiente and Ajax, he now plays for Lyon. While he is not particularly tall, he makes up for it with excellent timing and solid marking.

data.name: Gonzalo Montiel
data.description: The man who united a nation in tears of pure joy by converting the penalty that decided the 2022 World Cup final, and with a single kick became immortal. “It was a unique, dreamlike moment,” he said. “So many things flashed before my eyes, sacrifices my family and I had made to get me to this position. When I scored, I burst into tears. It was a unique moment that defied explanation.” A product of River Plate’s youth academy, he returned to the club in early 2025 and is now one of its key figures. Although he began his career as a centre-back, Marcelo Gallardo moved him to right-back due to his physical attributes – a decision that proved inspired. In addition to the 2022 World Cup, he also won both Copa América titles of the Scaloni era and the Finalissima. He has a son named Thiago, is fond of tattoos, and shows great personality on the pitch – and especially in shootouts. As a child he had such unusually prominent cheeks that he was given the nickname El Cachete – the Cheek – which has stuck with him.

data.name: Leandro Paredes
data.description: Paredes is a symbol of the Lionel Scaloni era with the national team. He was part of the generational shift when the coach arrived and has remained a constant presence ever since. A natural leader in the locker room, competitive and strong-willed, Paredes combines elegance, physicality and temperament on the pitch. He is known for his excellent vision and passing ability, as well as his strong ball recoveries. He came through the ranks at Boca Juniors, the club where he currently plays as captain, having returned there last year after 11 seasons in Europe that took him to teams including Roma, Paris Saint-Germain and Juventus. He is very close to key players in the squad such as Lionel Messi, Ángel Di María and Rodrigo De Paul, and is considered one of the team’s leaders. Beyond football, his other sporting passion is padel, which he plays frequently. “I play whenever I get the chance but I’m not the one who’s good at it,” Paulo Dybala said once. “Leandro’s the one who’s great, no doubt about it.”

data.name: Lisandro Martínez
data.description: Martínez is a highly valued defender for the national team. His aggressive style and strong character have earned him the nickname El Carnicero, the Butcher. Though he is not very tall – 1.75m (5ft 9in) – he is excellent in the air and complements this with great technique. He began his career at Newell’s Old Boys, stood out at Defensa y Justicia, and made the leap to Europe when he was transferred to Ajax aged 21. He joined Manchester United three years later. He is deeply connected to his country, to the point that he has the Qatar World Cup tattooed on his body and often posts about current events in Argentina. He shows this passion in every interview, where he also tends to express his emotions openly. His main weakness is injuries; he has suffered some serious setbacks that have prevented him from consistently maintaining a starting spot in the national team, and after suffering a knee ligament injury last year said there were times “I didn’t want to play any more … I felt empty”.

data.name: Rodrigo De Paul
data.description: Lionel Messi's footballing bodyguard. When he played for Racing and during his early years in Europe, De Paul was an attacking midfielder who stood out primarily for his creativity. But with the national team he has been transformed under Scaloni's guidance into a box-to-box player with great physical attributes – his nickname is Motorcito, Little Engine – key to the team's balance. As for his personality, he likes to visit his old neighborhood in Sarandí when he comes to Argentina, he's fun and outgoing (his humour is very important to the group), and he is a self-declared fashion fanatic: the varied looks he sports at each call-up often generate a lot of comments on social media. He's in a relationship with the famous Argentinian singer Tini Stoessel and plays for Inter Miami alongside his great friend Messi, after a four-year stint at Atlético Madrid.

data.name: Valentín Barco
data.description: Barco's story is one of overcoming adversity and of reinvention. Part of the Argentina team's generational shift, he was born in the town of Veinticinco de Mayo and, as a child, travelled 450km at least four times a week in his father’s Renault to train at Boca Juniors, which didn’t have room for him in its academy residence. That dedication eventually paid off: he made his first-team debut at 16 and excelled at Boca as a left-back, earning a move to Brighton, and he now shines at Strasbourg as a midfielder. “Having him in the squad, there is the option to play him at left-back, though right now he’s doing very well as a midfielder,” Scaloni said earlier this year. “He can play across the midfield and is a very good asset. Now he can carry a team on his shoulders. The team practically plays however he wants.”

data.name: Julián Álvarez
data.description: Born in the little village of Calchín, Córdoba, Álvarez worked tirelessly to become the star he is today. He had trials with Boca Juniors and Real Madrid aged just 11, and quickly made a name for himself at River Plate: at just 18 he was part of the squad that won the historic 2018 Copa Libertadores, and his footballing career exploded in 2021, when he scored 24 goals and provided 15 assists in 46 matches, lifting three titles. In doing so El Araña (the Spider) caught the eye of Lionel Scaloni, and he played a crucial role in the 2022 World Cup before moving to Manchester City, where he played for two seasons before he was signed by Atlético Madrid. His great goalscoring instinct, his intensity in pressing and his ability to link up with midfielders make him one of the most complete strikers in the world. While he was in Manchester his older brothers Rafael and Agustín played for Abbey Hey in the North West Counties League First Division South; since his move to Madrid they have signed for that city’s newly created Argentinian diaspora side, Club Argentino de Fútbol.

data.name: Lionel Messi
data.description: To become the best in the world (and, for many, the greatest in history), Messi had to spend his teenage years away from home. Since Newell's Old Boys couldn’t afford the growth hormone treatment he needed, he moved from Rosario to Barcelona, with his father, Jorge, at the age of 13 for a trial. By the time he left to join Paris Saint-Germain 21 years later, he had won seven Ballons d’Or and the hearts of the world. To his innate talent, he had to add a mental fortitude that proved crucial throughout his career – especially in never giving up after losing finals with the national team, which he endured at the 2014 World Cup and the 2015 and 2016 Copa Américas. This resilience ultimately led him to guide his country to two continental titles and to the pinnacle of success in Qatar. He will turn 39 during the group stage, and is now spending the final years of his professional career at Inter Miami, where he is also part of the club’s ownership group. There, he is surrounded by friends football has given him, such as Luis Suárez, and is enjoying his last dance while helping build a new era for the club, including a stadium that will feature a stand named in his honour.

data.name: Giovani Lo Celso
data.description: Giovani Lo Celso will finally have a chance at redemption after what happened in 2022, when he suffered a muscle injury shortly before the start of the World Cup and had to watch the team win the trophy without him. “Every kid dreams of being in the most beautiful tournament in the world. I tried everything I could, but there was no way,” he wrote at the time. Despite this setback, he still travelled with the team. He had already been part of the squad that won the 2021 Copa América and the Finalissima, and he was also included in the squad for the 2024 Copa América. A product of Rosario Central, he is a creative midfielder known for his excellent left foot and a level of talent that has taken him to clubs such as Paris Saint-Germain and Tottenham, as well as spells in Spain with Villarreal and Real Betis. Off the pitch, he is very family-oriented and maintains close friendships with Leandro Paredes and Ángel Di María.

data.name: Gerónimo Rulli
data.description: Rulli is a goalkeeper who combines technique with great resilience. Although he has spent much of his time with the national team in the shadow of Emi Martínez, his professionalism has turned him into a silent pillar of Lionel Scaloni’s era. He was part of the squads that won the Copa América in 2024, the Finalissima, and the World Cup in Qatar, where his role in supporting the group was fundamental. He emerged from Estudiantes de La Plata, where he broke clean-sheet records and caught everyone's attention at a young age, and had stints at major European teams such as Manchester City, Real Sociedad, Villarreal and Ajax before joining Marseille in 2024. Rulli is known for keeping a low profile and accepting supporting roles without losing his competitive edge, but at club level he is now a first choice and says the move to France has reignited his passion for the game. “I feel comfortable and happy,” he says. “I feel fulfilled in every way.”

data.name: Cristian Romero
data.description: Romero is one of the most respected figures in the current Argentina team. With an aggressive style of play and outstanding technical ability, he quickly earned the admiration of Argentinian fans and also of Tottenham, where he plays today and serves as the team captain. Nicknamed Cuti since infancy (because at the time his sister, Aldana, couldn’t say his actual name) he marries speed in anticipating plays with excellence in one-on-one duels with the added bonus of bringing a goal threat from set pieces. He emerged from the youth divisions of Club Atlético Belgrano de Córdoba, but his career took off in Italy, where he played for Genoa and Atalanta – but not, despite being on their books for a couple of years, Juventus – before making the leap to the Premier League. He was one of Lionel Scaloni’s key players in the conquests of the two Copa Américas, the Finalissima, and the World Cup in Qatar.

data.name: Exequiel Palacios
data.description: One of the most technically gifted midfielders to emerge from River Plate’s academy in recent years, Palacios was just 20 when he won the Copa Libertadores with his childhood club. Real Madrid came close to signing him, but injuries ultimately derailed the deal. Instead he joined Bayer Leverkusen in early 2020, with his current contract – extended last year – running to 2030. “Pala’s value to us has increased every season,” the club’s sporting director, Simon Rolfes, said. “His vast international experience as well as his outstanding strategic and individual qualities as a No 6 make him a key part of the team.” Born in Famaillá in Tucumán province, Palacios was welcomed back after the 2022 World Cup win to be declared "an illustrious citizen". “He is a role model for all the young people in the interior of the country, and especially for those from Tucumán,” said the vice-governor, Sergio Mansilla.

data.name: Nicolás González
data.description: Just as football brought González great joy with his two Copa América titles and the 2022 Finalissima, it also cruelly cost him his place at the Qatar World Cup, due to a muscle injury he suffered a few weeks before the tournament began. “It’s been my dream since I was a kid, and just when I was close to achieving it everything fell apart,” he said. This versatile, hard-working and prolific left-footed midfielder still plays a crucial role in Lionel Scaloni's side. His emergence in 2019 was surprising, and he quickly became one of the coach's favorite players. He developed and made his senior debut at Argentinos Juniors, made the leap to Europe with Stuttgart and then played for Fiorentina and Juventus before arriving at Atlético Madrid. He is a close friend of Alexis Mac Allister, as they are from the same age group and played together in the youth ranks at Argentinos Juniors.

data.name: Thiago Almada
data.description: Born in El Fuerte, the poor neighborhood of Buenos Aires that also produced Carlos Tevez, Almada started playing on the dirt fields of Club Santa Clara and today showcases his talent at Atlético Madrid, who paid €20m to bring him from Botafogo in 2025. He first came to attention as a youngster in Vélez Sarsfield's youth academy – and was named on the Guardian’s Next Generation shortlist in 2018 – and more so after he moved to Atlanta United and became one of the brightest talents in the MLS. He was part of the Argentina squad in Qatar at just 21, though he played for only six minutes, and has since become one of the most important players for the head coach, Lionel Scaloni. Nicolás Otamendi calls him “a leader”; the Greek striker Giorgos Giakomakis, a teammate at Atlanta, said: “Thiago’s talent is amazing. His skill, his vision, it’s incredible.” He is known for his great technical ability, and a style of play that is a clear demonstration of the Argentinian street football tradition.

data.name: Giuliano Simeone
data.description: Born in Rome because his father, Diego Simeone, was playing for Lazio at the time, Giuliano is nevertheless very Argentinian. He was brought up there and came up through River Plate's youth system until he was 16, when he moved to Atlético Madrid, the club that employ his father, and now benefits from his tireless work rate on the right wing. Inevitably he has faced down accusations of nepotism, people who told him he was in the team “only because I was Cholo’s son … people told me that a lot when I was a kid. Eventually it became normal to hear it. I don’t know if I’ve shut more than one mouth, but I’ve always tried to focus on myself. I’m a Simeone and I’m proud of my name, but I want to make a career under my own name, Giuliano.” He has also made Lionel Scaloni focus on him, and he is part of the national team’s process of generational renewal. He is demanding of himself (he says he watches every game again at home) and very close to his siblings, brothers Giovanni and Gianluca – both professional footballers – and sisters Francesca and Valentina. Football runs in the family.

data.name: Nico Paz
data.description: Paz was born in Tenerife because his father, the former Argentina defender Pablo Paz – who played in the 1998 World Cup – was based there at the time, but he never doubted which country he wanted to represent. Nico, one of the Albiceleste’s most promising talents, is one of the gems secured by Scaloni’s staff thanks to their extensive scouting work in Europe. They had been tracking him since his time in Real Madrid’s youth academy, and his first call-up came with the under-20s in 2022, when he was just 17. “I love both countries, but in the end, I decided to represent Argentina,” he said. “I think it’s the country that represents me the most, because of how football is lived there and because of my father. It’s an honour to follow in his footsteps.” He has just finished his second season at Como but Real still own half of his playing rights and could seek to bring back a player Francesco Totti has described as “original, creative and mischievous” and said could “return to Madrid and become one of the best in the world”.

data.name: Nicolás Otamendi
data.description: Otamendi may have grown to become one of the heroes of the national team but it has taken him time to reach that status, having been part of a generation that endured regular disappointment, alongside players such as Sergio Agüero, Javier Mascherano and even Lionel Messi, before the great redemption of 2022. After playing in the 2010 World Cup under Diego Maradona he was peripheral for several years, and was not picked in 2014, but this will be his fourth global tournament. A strong player with a huge personality, nicknamed El General because of his leadership (and his saluting goal celebration), he is captain and leader at Benfica. He is very good in the air, quick to close down opponents and physically strong. He carefully manages his diet (“I eat chicken, rice and salad practically every day, and only allow myself a little treat after matches”) and never stops training, even during his holidays. His tattoos are legendary. He got his first first at the age of 14 and they include Walter White, the protagonist of Breaking Bad, and Messi holding the World Cup trophy.

data.name: Alexis Mac Allister
data.description: Although he may go unnoticed off the pitch, on it he reliably catches the eye. A product of Argentinos Juniors' youth system and with a stint at Boca Juniors, he debuted for the Albiceleste in September 2019 and won the 2022 Finalissima, the World Cup, and the 2024 Copa América, in addition to standing out for the under-20 and under-23 teams. A great passer of the ball, versatile enough to play in different positions across the midfield, and with an eye for goal, he made the leap to Europe when he joined Brighton in 2019 and since 2023 has been playing for Liverpool, who paid €60m for him. He is the son of the former left-back Carlos Mac Allister and his brothers Kevin and Francis are both professional footballers. He likes to keep a diary and declares himself a "tattoo fanatic": he is decorated with, among other things, the names of his closest relatives, the date of his debut in the First Division, Jesus, a roulette wheel with the shirt numbers he has worn in his career and a drawing of himself as a child playing football.

data.name: José Manuel López
data.description: López’s path to the World Cup has been tough and anything but direct. As a youngster, after joining Lanús’s youth academy following his release from Independiente, he had a loan spell at Club Atlético Colegiales de Tres Arroyos, which competed in Argentina’s amateur regional leagues. He needed to regain his confidence in football, and that’s exactly what he did: he scored plenty of goals and, upon returning to Lanús, got a few chances with the first team. From there his reputation spread and, in 2022, at the age of 21 he was sold to Palmeiras, where he still plays. To overcome those ups and downs, his mental strength, humility and the values instilled in him by his parents were crucial. Born in San Lorenzo, he is the son of a father who worked as a fisherman, travelling between Mar del Plata and Patagonia. Nicknamed Flaco – Skinny – he made his debut for the national team in October 2025. He is a powerful striker with very good technical ability and a natural instinct for goal.

data.name: Lautaro Martínez
data.description: The second-highest scorer of Lionel Scaloni's era, behind only Leo Messi, Martínez has become one of the most important strikers in the history of the Argentina team. He played a key role in their Copa América victories in 2021 and 2024, as well as the 2022 Finalissima, although an ankle injury prevented him from showing his best at the 2022 World Cup. Born in Bahía Blanca, his career is the envy of any footballer: he began playing at Club Liniers in that city and then passed to Racing, where he progressed through the youth ranks and made his first-team debut. He has been playing for Inter for eight years, where he is the captain (winning two Serie A titles and reaching a Champions League final, among other achievements). His many tattoos include the motivational phrases "What doesn't kill me makes me stronger" and "Live the life you love. Love the life you live". His wife, Agustina Gandolfo, has posted on Instagram videos of Lautaro engaging in activities including playing the piano, drawing (which he resorts to when he can’t sleep) and doing the dusting.

data.name: Emiliano Martínez
data.description: Martínez is one of the most important and beloved players in the history of the Argentina team. He quickly won over the fans with his charisma and cunning on the pitch, in addition to his achievements as a two-time Copa América champion, a World Cup winner, and a Finalissima champion. His penalty shootout heroics and outstanding saves – such as the famous stop against Randal Kolo Muani in the World Cup final against France – have turned him into a fan idol. His effusive celebrations have gone viral worldwide and are often imitated by supporters. Nicknamed Dibu since childhood, because of his resemblance to the main character from the Argentinian animated series Mi Familia es un Dibujo (My Family is a Drawing), he began his career at Independiente but signed his first professional contract with Arsenal in the Premier League. His path was not easy, as he went through several loan spells before finally earning a move to his current club, Aston Villa – becoming the most expensive Argentinian goalkeeper in history in the process – and a starting spot in the national team. He is a player who openly expresses his emotions and brings attention to mental health, often highlighting the importance of working with a psychologist throughout his career.

data.name: Enzo Fernández
data.description: The speed with which Fernández integrated into the Argentina team is all the evidence you need of his immense talent: he made his debut just two months before the start of the 2022 World Cup, made the squad at 21 and with only three friendly matches under his belt, and by the end of the group stage he was in the first XI. Father of Olivia and Benjamín, he has played for Chelsea since late 2022 when the €121m they spent to bring him from Benfica made him the most expensive Argentinian player in history. He won titles with River Plate and during his loan spell at Defensa y Justicia, he enjoys fashion (he models for a clothing brand) and has tattoos on his back including a large lion, the World Cup trophy, and the phrase "God's timing is perfect". Less perfectly timed was his admission that he quite fancies a move to Madrid, which saw him dropped for a couple of games this past season. He was named Enzo in honor of Enzo Francescoli, a River Plate legend. In 2024 he apologised to his Chelsea teammates in a private message after he was caught on video chanting racist and homophobic slurs about members of the France team.

data.name: Facundo Medina
data.description: Medina never made his first-team debut for River Plate, yet he still holds the club close to his heart. In his own words, it lifted him out of the shantytown and helped shape him not only as a footballer but as a person, providing the tools that define him today as a high-quality left-footed defender. “It will always be my home because it’s where I was developed and raised,” he once said. Born in Villa Fiorito – the same neighborhood as Diego Maradona – he began his career as a left-back before transitioning to centre-back. He was soon transferred to Talleres, where he truly came into his own: he became a regular starter, earned a place in Argentina’s youth teams, and secured his move to Europe with Lens. Thanks to his profile and consistent performances, he quickly caught the attention of Lionel Scaloni, who handed him his senior debut at just 21. Now he is set to play in his first World Cup alongside several of the heroes of Qatar. This season, he has shown an excellent level while playing for Marseille.

data.name: Nahuel Molina
data.description: Argentina’s starting right-back at the last World Cup, Molina continues to hold that position. Emerging from the youth ranks of Boca Juniors, where he arrived at a very young age from his native Córdoba but never nailed down a place in the side, his career gathered momentum at Defensa y Justicia and Rosario Central, before he made the inevitable leap to Europe. Hard-working but quite introverted, and always faithful to his close friends and family, Molina’s career truly got liftoff at Udinese, opening the doors of the Albiceleste. Today, he is an Atlético Madrid player and became an elite full-back thanks to his speed and endurance. He stays away from controversies and is an infrequent user of social media. In January he celebrated the birth of his daughter, Allegra (with Barbara Occhiuzzi, his partner now for more than a decade). For Lionel Scaloni, he is a very important player in a difficult position.
